title.zh: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳
title.en: Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu
title.raw: 卷三十七 季布欒布田叔傳 Volume 37: Ji Bu, Luan Bu and Tian Shu

sentences.zh: 季布，楚人也，為任俠有名。

translations.literal: Ji Bu was a man of Chu; as a knight-errant he had a reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was a native of Chu who had won renown as a knight-errant.

sentences.zh: 項籍使將兵，數窘漢王。

translations.literal: Xiang Ji had him lead troops, and he repeatedly embarrassed the King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu put him in command of troops, and time and again he cornered the King of Han.

sentences.zh: 項籍滅，高祖購求布千金，敢有舍匿，罪三族。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was destroyed, Gaozu offered a thousand catties of gold for Ji Bu; whoever dared shelter or conceal him would be guilty to the third degree of kin.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Gaozu posted a reward of a thousand pounds of gold for Ji Bu, with death for three generations of kin for anyone who dared hide him.

sentences.zh: 布匿濮陽周氏，周氏曰：「漢求將軍急，跡且至臣家，能聽臣，臣敢進計；

translations.literal: Bu hid with the Zhou family of Puyang. The Zhou family said, "The Han search for the general is urgent; the trail will soon reach my house. If you can heed me, I dare advance a plan;
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu took refuge with the Zhou clan of Puyang. They told him, "The Han are hunting you relentlessly; their agents will soon be at my door. If you will trust me, I have a plan to propose;

sentences.zh: 即否，願先自剄。」

translations.literal: if not, I wish first to cut my own throat."
translations.idiomatic: if not, I would rather slit my own throat first."

sentences.zh: 布許之。

translations.literal: Bu assented to it.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu agreed.

sentences.zh: 乃髡鉗布，衣褐，置廣柳車中，并與其家僮數十人，之魯朱家所賣之。

translations.literal: Thereupon they shaved his head and put the iron collar on Bu, clothed him in coarse cloth, placed him in a broad-willow cart, together with several tens of his household slaves, and went to the place of Zhu Jia of Lu to sell him.
translations.idiomatic: They shaved his head, clamped him in the convict's collar, dressed him in a laborer's hemp, stowed him in a funeral cart with several dozen household servants, and drove him to Zhu Jia of Lu to be sold off as a slave.

sentences.zh: 朱家心知其季布也，買置田舍。

translations.literal: Zhu Jia in his heart knew he was Ji Bu; he bought him and placed him in a field house.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu Jia recognized him at once as Ji Bu, bought him, and lodged him on a country estate.

sentences.zh: 乃之雒陽見汝陰侯滕公，說曰：「季布何罪？

translations.literal: Thereupon he went to Luoyang to see the Marquis of Ru-yin, Lord Teng, and persuaded, saying, "What crime has Ji Bu?
translations.idiomatic: Then he traveled to Luoyang to see Xiahou Ying, the Marquis of Ru-yin, and argued, "What wrong has Ji Bu done?

sentences.zh: 臣各為其主用，職耳。

translations.literal: Ministers each for their lord are employed; it is duty only.
translations.idiomatic: Ministers serve their own lords—that is simply what duty requires.

sentences.zh: 項氏臣豈可盡誅邪？

translations.literal: The Xiang clan's ministers—how can they all be executed?
translations.idiomatic: Are we to execute every man who once served the house of Xiang?

sentences.zh: 今上始得天下，而以私怨求一人，何示不廣也！

translations.literal: Now the sovereign has just obtained the empire, yet by private resentment seeks one man—how this shows lack of breadth!
translations.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven has only just won the realm; to hound one man out of personal spite shows a narrowness ill befitting his position.

sentences.zh: 且以季布之賢，漢求之急如此，此不北走胡，南走越耳。

translations.literal: Moreover, by Ji Bu's worthiness, the Han's search for him being so urgent—this is nothing but fleeing north to the Hu or fleeing south to Yue.
translations.idiomatic: A man of Ji Bu's caliber, pressed this hard, will flee north to the Xiongnu or south to Nanyue—there is no other outcome.

sentences.zh: 夫忌壯士以資敵國，此伍子胥所以鞭荊平之墓也。

translations.literal: As for envying a brave man so as to aid an enemy state—this is why Wu Zixu whipped King Ping of Jing's tomb.
translations.idiomatic: To drive a brave man into the arms of a rival is the sort of blunder that made Wu Zixu flog the grave of King Ping of Chu.

sentences.zh: 君何不從容為上言之？」

translations.literal: Why do you not at ease for the sovereign speak of it?"
translations.idiomatic: Why not find a tactful moment and put this to the emperor yourself?"

sentences.zh: 滕公心知朱家大俠，意布匿其所，乃許諾。

translations.literal: Lord Teng in his heart knew Zhu Jia was a great knight-errant; he suspected Bu was hidden at his place; thereupon he promised.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Ying knew Zhu Jia for a famous man of chivalry and guessed Ji Bu lay hidden with him; he agreed to speak.

sentences.zh: 侍間，果言如朱家指。

translations.literal: Waiting for an interval, he indeed spoke as Zhu Jia had indicated.
translations.idiomatic: When a suitable occasion came, he repeated Zhu Jia's argument to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上乃赦布。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon pardoned Bu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor then granted Ji Bu a full pardon.

sentences.zh: 當是時，諸公皆多布能摧剛為柔，朱家亦以此名聞當世。

translations.literal: At this time, the various lords all praised Bu for being able to break hardness and make softness; Zhu Jia also by this had his name heard in that age.
translations.idiomatic: Men of rank admired Ji Bu for bending unyielding pride into survival, and Zhu Jia's name rang through the age for it.

sentences.zh: 布召見，謝，拜郎中。

translations.literal: Bu was summoned and seen; he thanked; he was appointed gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was summoned to court, thanked the emperor, and was appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 孝惠時，為中郎將。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaohui, he was general of the gentlemen of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Hui he rose to general of the palace gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 單于嘗為書嫚呂太后，太后怒，召諸將議之。

translations.literal: The Chanyu once made a letter insulting Empress Dowager Lü; the empress dowager was angry and summoned the various generals to deliberate on it.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu had once sent a letter that insulted Empress Dowager Lü; enraged, she called her generals in council.

sentences.zh: 上將軍樊噲曰：「臣願得十萬眾，橫行匈奴中。」

translations.literal: Senior General Fan Kuai said, "Your servant wishes to obtain a hundred thousand masses and rampage through the middle of the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, the senior general, declared, "Give me a hundred thousand men and I will sweep through the heart of the Xiongnu steppe."

sentences.zh: 諸將皆阿呂太后，以噲言為然。

translations.literal: The various generals all fawned on Empress Dowager Lü and took Kuai's words as correct.
translations.idiomatic: The generals, eager to please the empress dowager, pronounced Fan Kuai right.

sentences.zh: 布曰：「樊噲可斬也。

translations.literal: Bu said, "Fan Kuai can be beheaded.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu said, "Fan Kuai deserves execution.

sentences.zh: 夫以高帝兵三十餘萬，困於平城，噲時亦在其中。

translations.literal: As for by Gaozu's troops of more than three hundred thousand being trapped at Pingcheng, at that time Kuai also was within them.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu himself led over three hundred thousand men and was trapped at Pingcheng—and Fan Kuai was in that army.

sentences.zh: 今噲奈何以十萬眾橫行匈奴中，面謾！

translations.literal: Now how can Kuai with a hundred thousand masses rampage through the middle of the Xiongnu—lying to the face!
translations.idiomatic: How dare he now boast that a mere hundred thousand could ride roughshod over the Xiongnu? He is lying to your faces!

sentences.zh: 且秦以事胡，陳勝等起。

translations.literal: Moreover, Qin because of affairs with the Hu, Chen Sheng and others arose.
translations.idiomatic: Moreover, the Qin empire crumbled in part because it exhausted itself on the northern frontier, and Chen Sheng rose in rebellion.

sentences.zh: 今瘡痍未瘳，噲又面諛，欲搖動天下。」

translations.literal: Now the wounds are not yet healed, and Kuai again to the face flatters, wishing to shake and move the empire."
translations.idiomatic: The realm is still bleeding from those wars, and Fan Kuai flatters you to your face and would unsettle the whole empire again."

sentences.zh: 是時殿上皆恐，太后罷朝，遂不復議擊匈奴事。

translations.literal: At this time on the hall all were fearful; the empress dowager dismissed court and thereupon no longer deliberated on striking the Xiongnu affairs.
translations.idiomatic: A chill fell over the hall; the empress dowager adjourned court and never again brought up war on the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 布為河東守。

translations.literal: Bu was Hedong governor.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was appointed governor of Hedong.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，人有言其賢，召欲以為御史大夫。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, someone spoke of his worthiness; he was summoned with the intention of making him grandee secretary.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen, word of his talent reached the throne; he was summoned with a view to making him grandee secretary.

sentences.zh: 人又言其勇，使酒難近。

translations.literal: Someone again spoke of his bravery, that when drunk with wine he was hard to approach.
translations.idiomatic: Others countered that he was a brawler when drunk and impossible to work with.

sentences.zh: 至，留邸一月，見罷。

translations.literal: When he arrived, he was detained in the lodging-house one month and was dismissed on audience.
translations.idiomatic: He reached the capital, was kept a month in the official hostel, then sent away without appointment.

sentences.zh: 布進曰：「臣待罪河東，陛下無故召臣，此人必有以臣欺陛下者。

translations.literal: Bu advanced and said, "Your servant awaits punishment at Hedong; Your Majesty without reason summoned your servant—this person must have had something by which to deceive Your Majesty regarding your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu stepped forward and said, "I stand under sentence of fault at Hedong; Your Majesty summoned me without cause—someone must have oversold me to you.

sentences.zh: 今臣至，無所受事，罷去，此人必有毀臣者。

translations.literal: Now your servant has arrived, without receiving any affair, dismissed and sent away—this person must have had one who slandered your servant.
translations.idiomatic: Now that I am here, I am given no charge and dismissed—someone has blackened me behind my back.

sentences.zh: 夫陛下以一人譽召臣，一人毀去臣，臣恐天下有識者聞之，有以窺陛下。」

translations.literal: As for Your Majesty by one man's praise summoning your servant, by one man's slander sending away your servant—your servant fears the empire's knowing ones hearing it will have means to peer into Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: If one man's praise brings me to court and one man's slander sends me packing, thoughtful men everywhere will draw their own conclusions about Your Majesty's judgment."

sentences.zh: 上默然，慚曰：「河東吾股肱郡，故特召君耳。」

translations.literal: The sovereign was silent, ashamed, and said, "Hedong is my thigh-and-arm commandery; therefore I specially summoned you, that is all."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor fell silent, then said with embarrassment, "Hedong is one of my vital provinces; I merely wished to see you in person."

sentences.zh: 布之官。

translations.literal: Bu went to his office.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu returned to his post.

sentences.zh: 辯士曹丘生數招權顧金錢，事貴人趙談等，與竇長君善。

translations.literal: The persuader Cao Qiusheng several times summoned power and looked to money, served the noble men Zhao Tan and others, and was on good terms with Dou the elder lord.
translations.idiomatic: The rhetorician Cao Qiusheng traded on influence for gold, curried favor with men such as the favorite Zhao Tan, and was close to Dou Zhang, the empress's brother.

sentences.zh: 布聞，寄書諫長君曰：「吾聞曹丘生非長者，勿與通。」

translations.literal: Bu heard; he sent a letter remonstrating the elder lord, saying, "I have heard Cao Qiusheng is not a venerable elder; do not communicate with him."
translations.idiomatic: When Ji Bu heard of it, he wrote to Dou Zhang warning him, "Cao Qiusheng is no man of substance; have nothing to do with him."

sentences.zh: 及曹丘生歸，欲得書請布。

translations.literal: When Cao Qiusheng returned, he wished to obtain a letter to request Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Cao Qiusheng came home, he wanted a letter of introduction to Ji Bu.

sentences.zh: 竇長君曰：「季將軍不說足下，足下無往。」

translations.literal: Dou the elder lord said, "General Ji is not pleased with you; you must not go."
translations.idiomatic: Dou Zhang said, "General Ji dislikes you; you had better not go."

sentences.zh: 固請書，遂行。

translations.literal: He firmly requested the letter; thereupon he went.
translations.idiomatic: Cao pressed until Dou yielded a letter, then set out.

sentences.zh: 使人先發書，布果大怒，待曹丘。

translations.literal: He sent a man first to dispatch the letter; Bu indeed was greatly angry and awaited Cao Qiu.
translations.idiomatic: He had his letter sent ahead; Ji Bu flew into a rage and waited for Cao Qiusheng.

sentences.zh: 曹丘至，則揖布曰：「楚人諺曰『得黃金百，不如得季布諾』，足下何以得此聲梁楚之間哉？

translations.literal: When Cao Qiu arrived, then he bowed to Bu and said, "The men of Chu have a proverb: 'Obtaining yellow gold a hundred is not as good as obtaining Ji Bu's promise'—how did you obtain this reputation between Liang and Chu?
translations.idiomatic: When Cao arrived, he bowed to Ji Bu and said, "Chu has a saying: 'A hundred pounds of gold cannot buy Ji Bu's word'—how did you earn such fame across Liang and Chu?

sentences.zh: 且僕與足下俱楚人，使僕游揚足下名於天下，顧不美乎？

translations.literal: Moreover, your servant and you are both men of Chu; if your servant were to roam and raise your name in the empire—would it not be beautiful?
translations.idiomatic: We are both men of Chu; if I spread your name through the realm, would that not serve you well?

sentences.zh: 何足下距僕之深也！」

translations.literal: Why do you reject your servant so deeply!"
translations.idiomatic: Why do you shut me out so coldly!"

sentences.zh: 布乃大說。

translations.literal: Bu thereupon was greatly pleased.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu was delighted.

sentences.zh: 引入，留數月，為上客，厚送之。

translations.literal: He led him in, detained several months, made him upper guest, and richly sent him off.
translations.idiomatic: He brought him inside, kept him for months as an honored guest, and sent him away with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 布名所以益聞者，曹丘揚之也。

translations.literal: The reason Bu's name thereby increased in being heard was that Cao Qiu raised it.
translations.idiomatic: It was Cao Qiusheng's praise that made Ji Bu's name ring louder still.

sentences.zh: 布弟季心氣蓋關中，遇人恭謹，為任俠，方數千里，士爭為死。

translations.literal: Bu's younger brother Ji Xin, spirit covering Guanzhong, meeting people was respectful and careful; as knight-errant, square several thousand li, gentlemen strove to die for him.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's younger brother Ji Xin overshadowed all Guanzhong in sheer force of character; in person he was courteous and restrained, yet as a knight-errant his reputation ran for a thousand miles, and fighting men vied to die for him.

sentences.zh: 嘗殺人，亡吳，從爰絲匿，長事爰絲，弟畜灌夫、籍福之屬。

translations.literal: He once killed a man, fled to Wu, followed Ai Si in hiding, served Ai Si as elder, younger-brother-raised Guan Fu, Ji Fu's sort.
translations.idiomatic: After killing a man he fled to Wu and hid with Yuan Ang, whom he treated as an elder while treating Guan Fu, Ji Fu, and their like as younger brothers.

sentences.zh: 嘗為中司馬，中尉郅都不敢加。

translations.literal: He once was middle marshal; Commandant Zhi Du did not dare apply pressure.
translations.idiomatic: He served as a marshal of the center; even Commandant Zhi Du would not touch him.

sentences.zh: 少年多時時竊借其名以行。

translations.literal: Youths many times secretly borrowed his name to act.
translations.idiomatic: Young ruffians often invoked his name without permission.

sentences.zh: 當是時，季心以勇，布以諾，聞關中。

translations.literal: At this time, Ji Xin by bravery, Bu by promise, were heard of in Guanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: In those days Guanzhong knew Ji Xin for his courage and Ji Bu for keeping his word.

sentences.zh: 布母弟丁公，為項羽將，逐窘高祖彭城西。

translations.literal: Bu's mother's younger brother Ding Gong, being Xiang Yu's general, pursued and embarrassed Gaozu west of Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Ji Bu's maternal uncle Ding Gong, a general under Xiang Yu, had pressed Gaozu hard west of Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 短兵接，漢王急，顧謂丁公曰：「兩賢豈相厄哉！」

translations.literal: Short weapons met; the King of Han was in straits; looking back he said to Ding Gong, "Two worthies—how could they mutually obstruct!"
translations.idiomatic: In close combat the King of Han was desperate; turning to Ding Gong he cried, "Must two able men try to destroy each other?"

sentences.zh: 丁公引兵而還。

translations.literal: Ding Gong led the troops and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Gong withdrew his troops.

sentences.zh: 及項王滅，丁公謁見高祖，以丁公徇軍中，曰：「丁公為項王臣不忠，使項王失天下者也。」

translations.literal: When King Xiang was destroyed, Ding Gong had audience with Gaozu; by Ding Gong he displayed in the army, saying, "Ding Gong as King Xiang's minister was not loyal; he is one who made King Xiang lose the empire."
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Yu fell, Ding Gong came to pay his respects; Gaozu had him paraded before the host and declared, "Ding Gong was a faithless servant to his king; he helped cost Xiang Yu the empire."

sentences.zh: 遂斬之，曰：「使後為人臣無傚丁公也！」

translations.literal: Thereupon he beheaded him, saying, "Cause those after who are people's ministers not to imitate Ding Gong!"
translations.idiomatic: He had him executed, saying, "Let no future minister take Ding Gong as his model!"

sentences.zh: 欒布，梁人也。

translations.literal: Luan Bu was a man of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu came from Liang.

sentences.zh: 彭越為家人時，嘗與布游，窮困，賣庸於齊，為酒家保。

translations.literal: When Peng Yue was a commoner family man, he once roamed with Bu; in poverty and straits, he sold labor in Qi and was a wine-shop guarantor.
translations.idiomatic: When Peng Yue was still a commoner, he and Luan Bu had been companions; broke, they hired out as laborers in Qi and Luan tended a tavern.

sentences.zh: 數歲別去，而布為人所略，賣為奴於燕。

translations.literal: Several years they parted and went away, but Bu by people was abducted and sold as a slave in Yan.
translations.idiomatic: They parted for some years; then Luan Bu was kidnapped and sold into slavery in Yan.

sentences.zh: 為其主家報仇，燕將臧荼舉以為都尉。

translations.literal: For his master's household he avenged a wrong; the Yan general Zang Tu raised him and made him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He avenged his master's family on their enemies; the Yan general Zang Tu, impressed, appointed him a commandant.

sentences.zh: 荼為燕王，布為將。

translations.literal: When Tu became Yan king, Bu was general.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu became king of Yan, Luan Bu served as his general.

sentences.zh: 及荼反，漢擊燕，虜布。

translations.literal: When Tu rebelled, Han struck Yan and captured Bu.
translations.idiomatic: When Zang Tu rebelled, the Han attacked Yan and took Luan Bu prisoner.

sentences.zh: 梁王彭越聞之，乃言上，請贖布為梁大夫。

translations.literal: King of Liang Peng Yue heard it; thereupon he spoke to the sovereign, requesting to ransom Bu and make him Liang grandee.
translations.idiomatic: King Peng Yue of Liang heard of it and petitioned the emperor to ransom him and appoint him a grandee of Liang.

sentences.zh: 使於齊，未反，漢召彭越責以謀反，夷三族，梟首雒陽，下詔有收視者輒捕之。

translations.literal: He was sent on mission to Qi; before returning, Han summoned Peng Yue and reproached him with plotting rebellion, exterminated three clans, displayed the head at Luoyang, and issued an edict that whoever collected or viewed would immediately be arrested.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu was on embassy to Qi when, before he could return, the court summoned Peng Yue on a charge of treason, wiped out three generations of his kin, exposed his head in Luoyang, and decreed arrest for anyone who dared mourn or look upon him.

sentences.zh: 布還，奏事彭越頭下，祠而哭之。

translations.literal: Bu returned, reported affairs beneath Peng Yue's head, sacrificed and wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu returned, laid his report beneath Peng Yue's severed head, and offered sacrifice and tears.

sentences.zh: 吏捕以聞。

translations.literal: The clerks arrested and reported.
translations.idiomatic: The officials seized him and reported to the throne.

sentences.zh: 上召布罵曰：「若與彭越反邪？

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned Bu and reviled, saying, "You with Peng Yue rebelled, yes?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Luan Bu and shouted, "Were you in league with Peng Yue?

sentences.zh: 吾禁人勿收，若獨祠而哭之，與反明矣。

translations.literal: I forbade anyone to gather his remains or mourn him; you alone held sacrifice and wept for him—with rebellion the matter is already clear.
translations.idiomatic: I forbade anyone to claim his body or mourn him; you alone offered sacrifice and wept—your guilt is as plain as open rebellion.

sentences.zh: 趣亨之。」

translations.literal: Hasten boil him."
translations.idiomatic: Boil him at once."

sentences.zh: 方提趨湯，顧曰：「願一言而死。」

translations.literal: Just as being lifted and hurried to the soup, looking back he said, "I wish one word then die."
translations.idiomatic: As they dragged him toward the cauldron, he turned and said, "Let me speak one sentence before I die."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「何言？」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "What words?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked, "What is it?"

sentences.zh: 布曰：「方上之困彭城，敗滎陽、成皋間，項王所以不能遂西，徒以彭王居梁地，與漢合從苦楚也。

translations.literal: Bu said, "When Your Majesty was trapped at Pengcheng, defeated between Xingyang and Chenggao, the reason King Xiang could not thereupon proceed west was solely because Peng Wang dwelt in Liang territory, with Han joining alignment and suffering Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu said, "When you were besieged at Pengcheng and driven from Xingyang to Chenggao, the only reason Xiang Yu could not push west was that King Peng held Liang and kept Han and Chu locked in a stalemate that wore Chu down.

sentences.zh: 當是之時，彭王壹顧，與楚則漢破，與漢則楚破。

translations.literal: At this time, if Peng Wang had one glance—if with Chu then Han broken, if with Han then Chu broken.
translations.idiomatic: At that moment a single shift of Peng Yue's weight—if he had thrown in with Chu, Han would have shattered; if with Han, Chu would have shattered.

sentences.zh: 且垓下之會，微彭王，項氏不亡。

translations.literal: Moreover, at the Gaixia assembly, without Peng Wang, the Xiang clan would not have perished.
translations.idiomatic: And at Gaixia, without King Peng, the house of Xiang would never have fallen.

sentences.zh: 天下已定，彭王剖符受封，亦欲傳之萬世。

translations.literal: The empire already settled, Peng Wang split tallies and received investiture; he also wished to transmit it ten thousand generations.
translations.idiomatic: After peace was won, King Peng received his bronze tally like any other vassal king and hoped to pass his line down for ages.

sentences.zh: 今漢壹徵兵於梁，彭王病不行，而疑以為反。

translations.literal: Now Han once called up troops at Liang; Peng Wang was ill and did not go, yet was suspected and taken as rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: Now a single levy of troops from Liang, and because King Peng was too ill to march, you suspect him of treason.

sentences.zh: 反形未見，以苛細誅之，臣恐功臣人人自危也。

translations.literal: Rebellion form not yet seen, by harsh minuteness executing him—your servant fears the merit ministers each will self-fear.
translations.idiomatic: No sign of revolt appeared; to kill him on petty pretexts will make every man who helped you win the throne look over his shoulder.

sentences.zh: 今彭王已死，臣生不如死，請就亨。」

translations.literal: Now Peng Wang is already dead; your servant alive is not as good as dead; I request to approach the boil."
translations.idiomatic: King Peng is dead; I would rather join him in the cauldron than live on—throw me in."

sentences.zh: 上乃釋布，拜為都尉。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon released Bu and appointed him commandant.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor spared him and appointed him a commandant.

sentences.zh: 孝文時，為燕相，至將軍。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Xiaowen, he was Yan chancellor, reaching general.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen he served as chancellor of Yan and rose to general.

sentences.zh: 布稱曰：「窮困不能辱身，非人也；

translations.literal: Bu declared, saying, "In poverty and straits yet unable to humiliate the body—this is not a person;
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu used to say, "A man who will not endure indignity in poverty is no man at all;

sentences.zh: 富貴不能快意，非賢也。」

translations.literal: in wealth and honor unable to satisfy the will—this is not worthy."
translations.idiomatic: a worthy man who cannot have his way when fortune smiles is no worthy man."

sentences.zh: 於是嘗有德，厚報之；

translations.literal: Thereupon those on whom he once had virtue, he richly repaid them;
translations.idiomatic: Those who had done him kindness he repaid with generosity;

sentences.zh: 有怨，必以法滅之。

translations.literal: those with resentment, he invariably by law destroyed them.
translations.idiomatic: those who had wronged him he destroyed to the letter of the law.

sentences.zh: 吳楚反時，以功封為鄃侯，復為燕相。

translations.literal: When Wu and Chu rebelled, by merit he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Ju and again was Yan chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: During the rebellion of Wu and Chu he won a marquisate at Ju for his service and returned as chancellor of Yan.

sentences.zh: 燕齊之間皆為立社，號曰欒公社。

translations.literal: Between Yan and Qi all for him established altars, styled Luan Duke's altars.
translations.idiomatic: Between Yan and Qi the people set up shrines to him and called them the altars of Duke Luan.

sentences.zh: 布薨，子賁嗣侯，孝武時坐為太常犧牲不如令，國除。

translations.literal: Bu died; his son Ben inherited the marquisate; in the time of Emperor Xiaowu, sitting for as Grand Master of Ceremonies the sacrificial victims not according to ordinance, the state was removed.
translations.idiomatic: When Luan Bu died, his son Ben inherited the title; under Emperor Wu the fief was stripped after Ben, as Grand Master of Ceremonies, supplied defective sacrificial beasts.

sentences.zh: 田叔，趙陘城人也。

translations.literal: Tian Shu was a man of Zhao's Xingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu was a native of Xingcheng in Zhao.

sentences.zh: 其先，齊田氏也。

translations.literal: His ancestors were the Qi Tian clan.
translations.idiomatic: His clan was the old royal Tian house of Qi.

sentences.zh: 叔好劍，學黃老術於樂鉅公。

translations.literal: Shu liked swords; he studied Huang-Lao arts with Yue Ju the lord.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu loved fencing and studied Huang–Lao doctrine under Master Yue Ju.

sentences.zh: 為人廉直，喜任俠。

translations.literal: As a person he was incorrupt and straight, fond of knight-errantry.
translations.idiomatic: He was scrupulous, blunt-spoken, and drawn to the chivalrous life.

sentences.zh: 游諸公，趙人舉之趙相趙午，言之趙王張敖，以為郎中。

translations.literal: He roamed the various lords; Zhao people raised him to Zhao Chancellor Zhao Wu, who spoke to Zhao King Zhang Ao, and he was made gentleman of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He moved among the great houses until men of Zhao recommended him to Chancellor Zhao Wu, who brought him to King Zhang Ao of Zhao and had him appointed a gentleman of the palace.

sentences.zh: 數歲，趙王賢之，未及遷。

translations.literal: Several years; the Zhao king deemed him worthy, but before reaching promotion.
translations.idiomatic: For several years the king admired him but had not yet promoted him.

sentences.zh: 會趙午、貫高等謀弒上，事發覺，漢下詔捕趙王及群臣反者。

translations.literal: It happened that Zhao Wu, Guan Gao and others plotted to assassinate the sovereign; the affair was discovered; Han issued an edict to arrest the Zhao king and the various ministers who rebelled.
translations.idiomatic: Then Zhao Wu, Guan Gao, and their circle plotted regicide; when the plot was uncovered, the Han court ordered the arrest of King Zhang and every minister implicated.

sentences.zh: 趙有敢隨王，罪三族。

translations.literal: In Zhao whoever dared follow the king, crime to three clans.
translations.idiomatic: In Zhao, anyone who dared follow the king would die with three generations of his kin.

sentences.zh: 唯田叔、孟舒等十餘人赭衣自髡鉗，隨王至長安。

translations.literal: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu and more than ten others in crimson clothes themselves shaved head and iron collar, followed the king to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Only Tian Shu, Meng Shu, and a dozen others shaved their heads, donned the convict's collar and crimson robes, and followed their king to Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 趙王敖事白，得出，廢王為宣平侯，乃進言叔等十人。

translations.literal: Zhao King Ao's affair became clear, was able to come out, king was abolished and made Marquis of Xuanping, thereupon advanced speech the ten men including Shu.
translations.idiomatic: When King Zhang Ao's case was cleared he was released, stripped of his kingship, and ennobled as Marquis of Xuanping; he then recommended Tian Shu and the other nine men.

sentences.zh: 上召見，與語，漢廷臣無能出其右者。

translations.literal: The sovereign summoned and saw them, spoke with them; among Han court ministers none could go out to their right.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor received them in audience and spoke with them; not a single minister at the Han court could match their caliber.

sentences.zh: 上說，盡拜為郡守、諸侯相。

translations.literal: The sovereign was pleased; all were appointed commandery governors or feudal lords' chancellors.
translations.idiomatic: Pleased, the emperor appointed them all as commandery governors or chancellors to feudal kings.

sentences.zh: 叔為漢中守十餘年。

translations.literal: Shu was Hanzhong governor more than ten years.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu governed Hanzhong for over a decade.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝初立，召叔問曰：「公知天下長者乎？」

translations.literal: When Emperor Xiaowen first was established, he summoned Shu and asked, saying, "Sir, do you know the empire's venerable elders?"
translations.idiomatic: Soon after Emperor Wen took the throne, he summoned Tian Shu and asked, "Sir, can you name the worthiest men in the realm?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「臣何足以知之！」

translations.literal: He replied, saying, "Your servant how could be sufficient to know them!"
translations.idiomatic: He replied, "I am hardly fit to judge such matters."

sentences.zh: 上曰：「公長者，宜知之。」

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "You are a venerable elder; you ought to know."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "You are a man of weight yourself; you must have an opinion."

sentences.zh: 叔頓首曰：「故雲中守孟舒，長者也。」

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, "The former Yunzhong governor Meng Shu is a venerable elder."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "The former governor of Yunzhong, Meng Shu, is such a man."

sentences.zh: 是時孟舒坐虜大入雲中免。

translations.literal: At this time Meng Shu sat because barbarians greatly entered Yunzhong and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Meng Shu had just been dismissed because the Xiongnu had overrun Yunzhong in force.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「先帝置孟舒雲中十餘年矣，虜常一入，孟舒不能堅守，無故士卒戰死者數百人。

translations.literal: The sovereign said, "The late emperor placed Meng Shu in Yunzhong more than ten years already; barbarians often once entered, Meng Shu could not firmly defend; without reason soldiers who died in battle were several hundred.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "The late emperor kept Meng Shu at Yunzhong for more than ten years, yet when the raiders broke in he failed to hold the line, and hundreds of soldiers died for no good reason.

sentences.zh: 長者固殺人乎？」

translations.literal: Do venerable elders thus kill people?"
translations.idiomatic: Is that the conduct of a 'worthy elder'?"

sentences.zh: 叔叩頭曰：「夫貫高等謀反，天子下明詔，趙有敢隨張王者罪三族，然孟舒自髡鉗，隨張王，以身死之，豈自知為雲中守哉！

translations.literal: Shu knocked his head and said, "As for Guan Gao and others plotting rebellion, the Son of Heaven issued a clear edict that in Zhao whoever dared follow King Zhang would be guilty to three clans, yet Meng Shu himself shaved head and collar, followed King Zhang, with body would die for him—how could he have known he would become Yunzhong governor!
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu kowtowed and said, "When Guan Gao and his fellow plotters rose against the throne, the Son of Heaven decreed death for three generations of any Zhao official who followed King Zhang—yet Meng Shu shaved his head, took the collar, and followed his lord to the grave. How could he have dreamed he would one day govern Yunzhong?

sentences.zh: 漢與楚相距，士卒罷敝，而匈奴冒頓新服北夷，來為邊寇，孟舒知士卒罷敝，不忍出言，士爭臨城死敵，如子為父，以故死者數百人，孟舒豈敺之哉！

translations.literal: Han and Chu faced each other in stalemate; the troops were worn out, while the Xiongnu Modun had newly subdued the northern tribes and sent them raiding the border. Meng Shu knew his soldiers were exhausted and could not bring himself to order them out; yet the men vied to man the walls and die facing the foe, like sons for a father—so several hundred perished. How could Meng Shu have driven them to it!
translations.idiomatic: Han and Chu were deadlocked and the army was on its last legs, while Modun had just bent the northern tribes to his will and unleashed them on the frontier. Meng Shu knew his men were too spent to bear another order to battle—yet they stormed the walls like sons defending a father, and hundreds died. That was not Meng Shu driving them to their deaths.

sentences.zh: 是乃孟舒所以為長者。」

translations.literal: This then is why Meng Shu is what is called a venerable elder."
translations.idiomatic: That is precisely the mark of a true elder statesman."

sentences.zh: 於是上曰：「賢哉孟舒！」

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign said, "How worthy is Meng Shu!"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor exclaimed, "Meng Shu is a remarkable man!"

sentences.zh: 復召以為雲中守。

translations.literal: Again he was summoned and made Yunzhong governor.
translations.idiomatic: He was summoned back and reappointed governor of Yunzhong.

sentences.zh: 後數歲，叔坐法失官。

translations.literal: After several years, Shu by sitting law lost office.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later Tian Shu lost his post for a legal infraction.

sentences.zh: 梁孝王使人殺漢議臣爰盎，景帝召叔案梁，具得其事。

translations.literal: King Xiao of Liang sent men to kill Han councillor Yuan Ang; Emperor Jing summoned Shu to investigate Liang, completely obtained the affair.
translations.idiomatic: When King Xiao of Liang had the Han adviser Yuan Ang murdered, Emperor Jing sent Tian Shu to investigate the kingdom of Liang; he uncovered the full truth.

sentences.zh: 還報，上曰：「梁有之乎？」

translations.literal: Returning he reported; the sovereign said, "Did Liang have it?"
translations.idiomatic: On his return he was asked, "Did Liang do it?"

sentences.zh: 對曰：「有之。」

translations.literal: He replied, "There was it."
translations.idiomatic: He answered, "Yes."

sentences.zh: 「事安在？」

translations.literal: "Where is the affair?"
translations.idiomatic: "Where is the proof?"

sentences.zh: 叔曰：「上無以梁事為問也。

translations.literal: Shu said, "Your Majesty has nothing by which to take Liang affair as question.
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu said, "Your Majesty should not press the matter of Liang.

sentences.zh: 今梁王不伏誅，是廢漢法也；

translations.literal: Now if the King of Liang does not submit to execution, this is abandoning Han law;
translations.idiomatic: If the king of Liang goes unpunished, the law of the Han is void;

sentences.zh: 如其伏誅，太后食不甘味，臥不安席，此憂在陛下。」

translations.literal: if he submits to execution, the empress dowager will not find food sweet in eating, will not find peace in sleeping on mat—this worry is on Your Majesty."
translations.idiomatic: if he is executed, the empress dowager will eat without relish and sleep without rest—and that grief will be yours to bear."

sentences.zh: 於是上大賢之，以為魯相。

translations.literal: Thereupon the sovereign greatly deemed him worthy and made him Lu chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his judgment and appointed him chancellor to the king of Lu.

sentences.zh: 相初至官，民以王取其財物自言者百餘人。

translations.literal: When the chancellor first arrived at office, commoners who because the king took their goods spoke on their own were more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: Soon after the new chancellor took office, more than a hundred people came forward claiming the king had seized their property.

sentences.zh: 叔取其渠率二千人笞，怒之曰：「王非汝主邪？

translations.literal: Shu took their channel-leaders two thousand people and flogged them, angrily saying to them, "Is not the king your lord?
translations.idiomatic: Tian Shu seized the ringleaders among them—two thousand men—and had them flogged, shouting, "Is the king not your sovereign?

sentences.zh: 何敢自言主！」

translations.literal: How dare you on your own speak of the lord!"
translations.idiomatic: How dare you lodge complaints against your own lord!"

sentences.zh: 魯王聞之，大慚，發中府錢，使相償之。

translations.literal: The King of Lu heard it, was greatly ashamed, issued middle treasury money, had the chancellor repay them.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu was mortified; he drew funds from the inner treasury and told the chancellor to make restitution.

sentences.zh: 相曰：「王自使人償之，不爾，是王為惡而相為善也。」

translations.literal: The chancellor said, "Let the king himself send men to repay them; if not so, this is the king doing evil and the chancellor doing good."
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor replied, "Let Your Majesty's own agents pay them back; otherwise it will look as though the king did wrong and the minister claimed the credit."

sentences.zh: 魯王好獵，相常從入苑中，王輒休相就館。

translations.literal: The King of Lu liked hunting; the chancellor always followed entering the park; the king always rested the chancellor to go to lodging.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Lu loved the chase; the chancellor always accompanied him into the royal park, whereupon the king would send him off to the lodge to rest.

sentences.zh: 相常暴坐苑外，終不休，曰：「吾王暴露，獨何為舍？」

translations.literal: The chancellor always exposed sat outside the park, to the end not resting, saying, "My king is exposed to the elements; how alone should I go to lodging?"
translations.idiomatic: The chancellor would sit in the open outside the park and refuse to leave, saying, "While my king endures wind and weather, why should I seek shelter?"

sentences.zh: 王以故不大出遊。

translations.literal: The king for this reason did not much go out roaming.
translations.idiomatic: After that the king rarely went out on long hunting trips.

sentences.zh: 數年以官卒，魯以百金祠，少子仁不受，曰：「義不傷先人名。」

translations.literal: Several years later he died in office; Lu with a hundred in gold sacrificed; the youngest son Ren did not accept, saying, "By righteousness I will not harm the former man's name."
translations.idiomatic: He died in office some years later. Lu offered his youngest son, Ren, a hundred catties of gold for the funeral, but Ren refused, saying, "Duty forbids my tarnishing my father's reputation."

sentences.zh: 仁以壯勇為衛將軍舍人，數從擊匈奴。

translations.literal: Ren by stalwart bravery was the General of Wei's retainer, several times followed striking the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: Ren, known for his courage, became a retainer of the General of the Guards and repeatedly campaigned against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 衛將軍進言仁為郎中，至二千石、丞相長史，失官。

translations.literal: The General of Wei advanced speech making Ren a gentleman of the palace; he reached two thousand piculs, chancellor chief clerk, lost office.
translations.idiomatic: The general recommended him for a gentleman of the palace; he rose to two-thousand-bushel rank as chief clerk to the chancellor, then lost his post.

sentences.zh: 後使刺三河，還，奏事稱意，拜為京輔都尉。

translations.literal: Later he was sent to investigate the Three He; returning, reporting affairs suited the intent; he was appointed metropolitan commandant.
translations.idiomatic: Later he was dispatched as inspector over the Three He region; his report pleased the throne, and he was named metropolitan commandant of the capital districts.

sentences.zh: 月餘，遷司直。

translations.literal: After more than a month, he was promoted straight superintendent.
translations.idiomatic: Within a month he was promoted to superintendent of trials.

sentences.zh: 數歲，戾太子舉兵，仁部閉城門，令太子得亡，坐縱反者族。

translations.literal: Several years later the heir apparent Li raised troops; Ren, whose duty was to secure the gates, failed to hold them; the crown prince was able to flee; he was sentenced for letting a rebel escape and his whole clan was executed.
translations.idiomatic: Some years later, when Crown Prince Li mutinied, Tian Ren, who commanded the gate guard, failed to seal the capital in time and allowed the prince to break out; he was convicted of abetting a traitor, and his entire clan perished with him.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：以項羽之氣，而季布以勇顯名楚，身履軍搴旗者數矣，可謂壯士。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: By Xiang Yu's fierce spirit, yet Ji Bu by bravery displayed name in Chu; personally in the army seizing banners—several times—can be called a brave warrior.
translations.idiomatic: The historian's verdict: In an age dominated by Xiang Yu's fury, Ji Bu won fame in Chu for raw courage; he repeatedly charged into battle and tore enemy banners from their poles—a true fighting man.

sentences.zh: 及至困厄奴僇，苟活而不變，何也？

translations.literal: When it came to straits and humiliation as a slave, living ignobly yet not changing—why?
translations.idiomatic: Yet when he was broken, enslaved, and humiliated, he clung to life without yielding his purpose—why was that?

sentences.zh: 彼自負其材，受辱不羞，欲有所用其未足也，故終為漢名將。

translations.literal: He himself bore his talents; receiving humiliation he was not ashamed, wishing to have something to use of what was not yet sufficient; therefore in the end he became a Han famous general.
translations.idiomatic: He trusted his own abilities; shame did not break him because he still meant to put his gifts to use. In the end he became one of the Han's celebrated generals.

sentences.zh: 賢者誠重其死。

translations.literal: The worthy truly value their death.
translations.idiomatic: A worthy man weighs his death with utmost care.

sentences.zh: 夫婢妾賤人，感概而自殺，非能勇也，其畫無俚之至耳。

translations.literal: As for maidservants and mean persons, stirred in feeling and killing themselves—it is not able to be courage; their plan crude reaches the utmost only.
translations.idiomatic: Maids and common folk who work themselves into a passion and take their own lives show no courage—only the shallowest recklessness.

sentences.zh: 欒布哭彭越，田叔隨張敖，赴死如歸，彼誠知所處，雖古烈士，何以加哉！

translations.literal: Luan Bu wept for Peng Yue; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao; they went to death like returning home—they truly knew their place; even ancient steadfast martyrs—how could one add more!
translations.idiomatic: Luan Bu mourned Peng Yue at the risk of his life; Tian Shu followed Zhang Ao into chains as if going home. They knew exactly where they stood; the steadfast heroes of old could scarcely have surpassed them.
